g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Document-Based NoSQL Systems and MongoDB, the leading document database for modern applications.
NoSQL databases emerged to address big data challenges, offering flexible schemas and horizontal scalability for modern applications.
MongoDB is the leading document-based NoSQL database storing data as flexible JSON-like BSON documents without predefined schemas.
g.text: Stores data as BSON (Binary JSON) documents with dynamic schemas
Provides horizontal scaling via sharding and high availability via replica sets

g.data-narration: MongoDB architecture includes mongod server process, mongos router for sharded clusters, and config servers storing cluster metadata.
MongoDB organizes data hierarchically: databases contain collections holding flexible BSON documents with unique _id primary keys.
BSON is Binary JSON - MongoDB's storage format supporting additional types like Date, ObjectId, Binary data beyond standard JSON.
g.text: BSON Types: String, Number, Boolean, Date, ObjectId, Binary, Array, Object, Null

g.data-narration: MongoDB provides CRUD operations: insertOne/Many for creation, find for reading, updateOne/Many and delete for modification.
MongoDB supports rich query operators: comparison operators like $gt, $lt, $eq; logical operators like $and, $or; array operators like $in, $all.
Indexes in MongoDB are B-tree structures that improve query performance by allowing fast document lookup without full collection scans.
g.text: Use explain() to analyze query execution plans

g.data-narration: MongoDB supports single-field, compound, multikey for arrays, text indexes for search, and hashed indexes for sharding distribution.
The aggregation framework processes documents through pipeline stages like $match, $group, $project, $sort for data transformation.
MongoDB replica sets provide high availability with one primary and multiple secondary nodes, using oplog for automatic data replication.
Sharding enables horizontal scaling by distributing data across shards using a shard key, with mongos routing queries to appropriate shards.
MongoDB differs from SQL: flexible schemas vs rigid tables, eventual vs strong consistency, horizontal vs vertical scaling approaches.
Key takeaways: MongoDB uses BSON documents with flexible schemas, provides indexing for performance, aggregation for analytics, replica sets for availability.
Exam tips: Remember BSON extends JSON, _id defaults to ObjectId, aggregate $match early, choose high-cardinality shard keys to avoid hot-spotting.
g.text: CAP Theorem: MongoDB prioritizes Availability & Partition Tolerance (AP) over Consistency
